content.text: Screen Reader Testing for GenAI Output Formats

GenAI applications produce a wider variety of output formats than ordinary web pages: streamed prose, rendered Markdown, fenced code blocks, data tables, and mathematical formulas. Each format has its own accessibility failure mode when read aloud by a screen reader. This sub-subtopic covers what breaks with NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, and Orca for each output type, how to reproduce those failures in automated tests, and how to structure a per-release manual test protocol.

The core challenge is that GenAI output is generated dynamically. Markup must be in the DOM before the screen reader can read it, and the live region strategy must balance timely announcements against interrupting the user who is already listening to earlier content.

attrs.alt: Screen reader interaction with four GenAI output formats and a five-step release test protocol

content.text: How Each Output Format is Announced
Streamed text should live inside a container marked with
policy queues announcements and waits for the user to finish listening before reading new chunks. Setting
on streaming output is the single most common mistake: it interrupts the user every few hundred milliseconds and makes the application unusable with a screen reader.
attribute tells the reader to announce only text additions — not removals, which would fire on every DOM reconciliation patch.
The most important rule: never inject raw Markdown strings into the DOM.
is read as "asterisk asterisk bold asterisk asterisk" by all major screen readers. Always pass the model response through a Markdown renderer (
, or a framework component) before inserting HTML.
Fenced code blocks need a language label so the reader announces context before reading the code. Add the label as

def hello():
        return "world"
, JAWS reads the opening curly-brace of a JSON block as "left brace" and continues reading every symbol character. The label gives the user a chance to navigate past with a single keystroke instead.
When the model outputs structured data (comparisons, schedules, metrics), the Markdown table must be converted to a semantic HTML
attribute to announce the column or row header before each cell during table navigation.

Model comparison: latency vs cost
Model p95 latency (ms) Cost per 1M tokens ($)
GPT-4o 1 200 5.00
Claude 3.5 Sonnet 980 3.00
element provides an announcement when the screen reader enters the table: "Table: Model comparison: latency vs cost, 2 rows, 3 columns."
KaTeX renders formulas to HTML + MathML, but browser/reader support for MathML is inconsistent. NVDA + Firefox reads MathML natively; JAWS + Chrome does not. The safest approach adds an
with a human-readable description as a fallback:
For inline formulas inside a prose response, the aria-label should be a complete sentence describing the formula's meaning, not just a reading of the symbols.
Pure automated tests cannot run NVDA or JAWS in a browser — those are OS-level processes. What you can automate is verifying that the
regions are wired correctly and that the DOM announces the right content. Use Playwright's accessibility snapshots to assert on the accessibility tree state after the model response appears.

# test_screen_reader_sim.py
import pytest
from playwright.sync_api import Page, expect

BASE = "http://localhost:5173"


@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def page(browser):
    ctx = browser.new_context()
    pg = ctx.new_page()
    pg.goto(BASE)
    yield pg
    ctx.close()


def test_live_region_policy_is_polite(page: Page):
    """aria-live must be polite, not assertive, on the message list."""
    msg_list = page.locator("#message-list")
    assert msg_list.get_attribute("aria-live") == "polite"
    assert msg_list.get_attribute("role") == "log"


def test_thinking_indicator_announced(page: Page):
    """Thinking indicator must use role=status so SR announces it without focus."""
    indicator = page.locator("#thinking-indicator")
    assert indicator.get_attribute("role") == "status"
    assert indicator.get_attribute("aria-live") == "polite"


def test_code_block_has_language_label(page: Page):
    """Code blocks must have aria-label containing the language name."""
    page.locator("#chat-input").fill("Write a Python hello-world function.")
    page.locator("#send-btn").click()
    # Wait for response to contain a code block
    code_block = page.locator("pre[aria-label]").first
    code_block.wait_for(timeout=30_000)
    label = code_block.get_attribute("aria-label") or ""
    assert "python" in label.lower(), f"Expected language in aria-label, got: {label!r}"


def test_table_has_column_headers(page: Page):
    """Model-generated tables must use <th scope=col> for screen reader nav."""
    page.locator("#chat-input").fill("Compare GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet in a table.")
    page.locator("#send-btn").click()
    table = page.locator("table").first
    table.wait_for(timeout=30_000)
    headers = table.locator("th[scope='col']")
    expect(headers).to_have_count(at_least=2)


def test_accessibility_tree_snapshot(page: Page, snapshot):
    """Accessibility tree of the chat area matches the approved snapshot."""
    tree = page.accessibility.snapshot(root=page.locator("#message-list"))
    assert snapshot == tree
test uses pytest-snapshot. On the first run it writes the snapshot; subsequent runs fail if the accessibility tree changes unexpectedly.

Automated tests verify structure; they cannot verify what the user actually hears. Run the manual protocol below with a real screen reader before every release that touches the chat UI. Assign a dedicated tester — the developer who wrote the feature should not also be the tester.
Page title, then landmark: "AI Assistant, main"
Type a question and press Enter
"Thinking" announced by status region within 1 s
Response text read automatically without user action
"Python code" (or matching lang label) before code text
"Table: [caption], N rows, M columns" on entry
Send a message then press Stop
Navigate to prior messages with arrow keys
Each message read with speaker label (You / AI)
All text and icons remain visible, no invisible elements
Record results in a shared spreadsheet linked from the release notes. Any fail blocks the release.
Speaker Label Pattern for Message History
When the user navigates up through the conversation history with the screen reader's arrow keys, each message must be identifiable as coming from the user or the AI. Use

What is the capital of France?

Paris is the capital of France. It has been the capital since 987 AD.

on the AI message should summarise the full response for short replies; for long responses, truncate after 200 characters so the reader does not block on a five-paragraph essay while the user is trying to navigate.
after streaming completes, not during, to avoid the reader re-announcing a growing string every second:
Run the automated accessibility tests in CI on every pull request. Add a Lighthouse CI check for accessibility score alongside the axe-core Playwright tests:

# .github/workflows/a11y.yml
name: Accessibility

on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  axe:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: "20" }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx playwright install --with-deps chromium
      - run: npm run dev &
      - run: npx wait-on http://localhost:5173
      - run: pytest tests/a11y/ -v
        env: { PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL: "http://localhost:5173" }

  lighthouse:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: "20" }
      - run: npm ci && npm run build
      - run: npx serve dist &
      - run: npx wait-on http://localhost:3000
      - name: Lighthouse CI
        uses: treosh/lighthouse-ci-action@v11
        with:
          urls: "http://localhost:3000"
          configPath: ".lighthouserc.json"

# .lighthouserc.json
# {
#   "ci": {
#     "assert": {
#       "assertions": {
#         "categories:accessibility": ["error", {"minScore": 0.95}]
#       }
#     }
#   }
# }

Modern GenAI assistants often return more than text — images generated inline, citation footnotes with links to sources, and collapsible reasoning traces. Each of these needs its own accessibility treatment.
When the model returns a generated image (via tool call to an image API), the
attribute derived from the model's description of the image, not a generic placeholder like "AI-generated image":
The model's image-generation tool response typically includes a
field — use that verbatim as the alt attribute, trimmed to 125 characters for WCAG compliance.
RAG-backed assistants often add inline citations like
linking to source documents. These are visually recognisable but meaningless to a screen reader without extra context:
[1] [1] [1]: AWS documentation on VPC networking
CSS class hides text visually while keeping it in the accessibility tree (
position: absolute; width: 1px; height: 1px; overflow: hidden; clip: rect(0,0,0,0);
Chain-of-thought or extended thinking outputs are often rendered in a collapsible
element. Screen readers announce these natively:
Show reasoning
element is natively accessible in all major screen readers — pressing Enter or Space on the
element toggles the expanded state. Do not replace it with a custom
click handler unless you replicate all ARIA semantics.
Different screen reader and browser combinations have significantly different behaviour on the same HTML. The following matrix captures the coverage required before shipping a GenAI chat UI:
Test at minimum: NVDA + Chrome, JAWS + Chrome, VoiceOver + Safari, and Orca + Firefox. Mobile (TalkBack + Chrome Android) should be covered if the app has a mobile web view.
Implementing a Feedback Loop for Screen Reader Bugs
Because most developers test with mouse and keyboard but not screen readers, bugs in this area often survive to production. Add a low-friction mechanism for users to report accessibility issues:
Report accessibility issue
Track accessibility bugs in their own label (
) in your issue tracker and target a 2-sprint resolution SLA for WCAG AA violations. Each closed bug should include a regression test so it cannot reappear.
Every fixed screen reader bug should become a named test case:

# test_sr_regressions.py
# Each test documents the bug it guards against.

def test_nvda_chrome_live_region_not_assertive(page):
    """
    Bug: live region was assertive, interrupting users every 200ms.
    Fixed: changed to polite. Guards against reverting to assertive.
    """
    msg_list = page.locator("#message-list")
    assert msg_list.get_attribute("aria-live") == "polite"


def test_code_block_no_raw_asterisks(page):
    """
    Bug: NVDA read '**bold**' as 'asterisk asterisk bold asterisk asterisk'
    because Markdown was injected raw.
    Fixed: markdown-it renders to HTML before DOM insertion.
    """
    page.locator("#chat-input").fill("Make the word 'hello' bold in Markdown.")
    page.locator("#send-btn").click()
    response = page.locator(".ai-message").last
    response.wait_for(timeout=20_000)
    # Raw asterisks must not appear in the rendered DOM
    text_content = response.inner_text()
    assert "**" not in text_content, f"Raw Markdown found in response: {text_content!r}"


def test_table_caption_present(page):
    """
    Bug: model-generated tables had no caption, so SR said 'table, 3 columns'
    with no context. Fixed: post-processor adds caption from preceding heading.
    """
    page.locator("#chat-input").fill("Show me a table comparing SQL vs NoSQL.")
    page.locator("#send-btn").click()
    table = page.locator("table").first
    table.wait_for(timeout=30_000)
    caption = table.locator("caption")
    assert caption.count() == 1, "Table must have a <caption> for screen readers"
Screen reader accessibility for GenAI UIs requires treating each output format separately. Streamed prose needs
container. Markdown must be rendered to HTML before injection. Code blocks need
with the programming language. Tables need
fallback for readers that do not support MathML.
— for streaming output to avoid interrupting the user
Never inject raw Markdown: render to HTML first so that symbols are not read verbatim
with the language name on every code block so users can navigate past it
Run the 10-step manual test protocol with NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, and Orca before each release
